summary:
The English Bill of Rights (December 16, 1689 AD) was a landmark constitutional document produced by Parliament following the [[event:2330|Glorious Revolution]] that formally established the constitutional monarchy in England, limiting royal prerogative and guaranteeing parliamentary sovereignty and individual civil rights — one of the foundational texts of liberal democratic constitutionalism. Offered to William III and Mary II as a condition of accepting the throne, the Bill reaffirmed and extended the [[event:2232|Magna Carta's]] tradition of constraining royal power through law and custom. Its provisions directly responded to the specific abuses of the Stuart kings: the prohibition on maintaining a standing army in peacetime without parliamentary consent (responding to Charles II's and James II's use of standing armies to intimidate Parliament); the requirement of parliamentary consent for taxation; the freedom of elections and parliamentary debate; and the prohibition on 'cruel and unusual punishments' (a phrase copied almost verbatim into the American Bill of Rights a century later).

The Bill also settled the religious question at the heart of the English Civil War and Glorious Revolution: it prohibited any Catholic, or anyone married to a Catholic, from inheriting the English throne — a provision that remains nominally in force today (modified by the 2013 Succession to the Crown Act to allow marriage to Catholics while still prohibiting Catholic monarchs). The Bill of Rights, combined with the Act of Settlement (1701) and the [[event:2365|Act of Union]] (1707), created the constitutional framework of the British state. Its influence on the [[event:2350|American Declaration of Independence]], the [[event:2396|US Constitution]], and the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights makes it one of the most influential constitutional documents in history.
